name: Donald Trump
summary: Donald Trump's character and pattern of behavior are marked by a history of dishonesty, racism, and a disregard for the law. He has repeatedly made false claims, such as lying about his academic record and financial success, and has been involved in numerous lawsuits and bankruptcies. Trump has also demonstrated a willingness to exploit and harm vulnerable groups, including minorities, immigrants, and women, through his words and actions. His presidency was marked by a consistent effort to undermine democratic institutions, ignore scientific consensus, and prioritize his own interests over the well-being of the country. Trump's actions have resulted in significant harm to individuals and communities, and his legacy is one of division, corruption, and abuse of power.

entries.fact: Trump falsely claimed he was top of his class with the highest grades possible at the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania
Donald Trump countersued the U.S. government for $100 million over charges that his properties had discriminated against Black applicants and tenants, with the help of his lawyer and mentor Roy Cohn.
The case was settled with a consent decree agreeing to desegregate, which the Trumps ended up violating four years later.
Trump paid Holiday $70 million to take sole control of Harrah's at Trump Plaza, a hotel and casino in Atlantic City, after it was unprofitable.
Trump filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for six of his businesses, including the Plaza Hotel in Manhattan and the casinos in Atlantic City, New Jersey.
Trump filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for the Trump Taj Mahal casino in Atlantic City.
Trump defaulted on his loans for the Trump Shuttle airline and ownership passed to the banks.
The Plaza Hotel filed for bankruptcy protection, and a reorganization plan was approved a month later, with the banks taking control of the property.
Trump's casinos, including the Trump Plaza and Trump Castle, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection.

Donald Trump defaulted on over $3 billion of bank loans, and the lenders seized the Plaza Hotel along with most of his other properties in a 'vast and humiliating restructuring' that allowed him to avoid personal bankruptcy.
Trump founded Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR), which assumed ownership of the Trump Plaza and later purchased the Taj Mahal and Trump Castle, but went bankrupt in 2004 and 2009.

Trump declared the Trump International Hotel and Tower in Chicago to be worthless on his tax return, despite later being investigated by the IRS for potentially writing off losses incurred through construction cost overruns and lagging sales.

sources.name: The New York Times and ProPublica

entries.fact: Trump University changed its name to the Trump Entrepreneur Initiative after New York State authorities notified the company that its use of 'university' violated state law
NBC and Univision dropped the Miss Universe pageants in reaction to Trump's comments about Mexican immigrants
Donald Trump imposed a travel ban on seven Muslim-majority countries as part of his immigration policy.
Donald Trump expanded the Mexico–United States border wall as part of his immigration policy.
Donald Trump enforced a family separation policy on the border as part of his immigration policy.
Donald Trump had been involved in more than 4,000 lawsuits, liens, and other filings, often filed for nonpayment against him by employees, contractors, real estate brokers, and his own attorneys.
Trump declared the Mar-a-Lago estate in Palm Beach, Florida, as his primary residence.
Trump was impeached for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress
Donald Trump downplayed the severity of the COVID-19 pandemic and contradicted health officials
Trump was impeached for incitement of insurrection
Trump's attempt to overturn the 2020 presidential election result culminated in the January 6 Capitol attack
Trump was found liable in civil cases for sexual abuse and defamation and for business fraud
Trump initiated mass layoffs of federal workers at the start of his second presidency
Trump imposed tariffs on nearly all countries at the highest level since the Great Depression
Trump's administration targeted political opponents and civil society, persecuted transgender people, and conducted mass deportation of immigrants
Trump was found guilty in 34 counts of falsifying business records, making him the first U.S. president convicted of a felony

The New York Times and ProPublica reported that the Internal Revenue Service was investigating Trump for potentially writing off losses incurred through construction cost overruns and lagging sales of residential units in the Trump International Hotel and Tower in Chicago.

sources.name: The New York Times and ProPublica

entries.fact: Trump authorized a joint U.S./Israeli war with Iran that resulted in the killing of Iran's leader
The State of New York filed a $40 million civil suit against Trump University, alleging that the company made false statements and defrauded consumers
Donald Trump promised to release his tax returns if he ran for office, but later failed to do so

Donald Trump announced his candidacy for the 2016 presidential election, campaigning as a rich, successful businessman and an outsider without political experience, and claimed media bias against him.
Trump agreed to pay a total of $25 million to settle three cases related to Trump University
The New York attorney general stated that the Donald J. Trump Foundation had violated state law by soliciting donations without submitting to required annual external audits
Trump became the Republican front-runner in March 2016 and was declared the presumptive Republican nominee in May.
Trump's campaign platform emphasized renegotiating U.S.-China relations and free trade agreements such as NAFTA and strongly enforcing immigration laws.
Trump advocated for increasing military spending and extreme vetting or banning of immigrants from Muslim-majority countries.
Trump promised to build a wall on the Mexico-U.S. border and vowed that Mexico would pay for it.
Trump pledged to deport millions of illegal immigrants residing in the U.S.
Trump criticized birthright citizenship for incentivizing 'anchor babies'.
Trump's campaign launch speech drew criticism for claiming Mexican immigrants were 'bringing drugs, they're bringing crime, they're rapists'.
Donald Trump rejected the scientific consensus on climate change
Donald Trump reduced the budget for renewable energy research by 40 percent
Donald Trump reversed Obama-era policies directed at curbing climate change
Donald Trump withdrew from the Paris Agreement, making the U.S. the only nation to not ratify it
Donald Trump rolled back more than 100 federal environmental regulations, including those that curbed greenhouse gas emissions, air and water pollution, and the use of toxic substances
Donald Trump signed an executive order authorizing procedures for repealing the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act (Obamacare) on his first day in office
Donald Trump signed an executive order withdrawing from the Trans-Pacific Partnership negotiations on his first day in office
Donald Trump signed an executive order advancing the Keystone XL and Dakota Access Pipeline projects on his first day in office
Donald Trump signed an executive order planning for a wall along the U.S. border with Mexico on his first day in office
Donald Trump signed the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, which reduced tax rates for businesses and individuals and eliminated the penalty associated with the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate
The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 increased the federal budget deficit by almost 50 percent, to nearly $1 trillion in 2019
The New York attorney general's office filed a civil suit against the Donald J. Trump Foundation, Trump, and his adult children, seeking $2.8 million in restitution and additional penalties
The U.S. national debt increased by 39 percent under Donald Trump, reaching $27.75 trillion, and the U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio hit a post-World War II high
A New York state judge ordered Trump to pay $2 million to a group of charities for misusing the foundation's funds, in part to finance his presidential campaign

Trump weakened protections for animals and environmental standards for federal infrastructure projects, and expanded permitted areas for drilling and resource extraction, such as allowing drilling in the Arctic Refuge.

Trump dismantled federal regulations on health, labor, the environment, and other areas, including a bill that revoked the Obama-era regulation restricting the sale of firearms to severely mentally ill people.
Trump delayed, suspended, or reversed ninety federal regulations during his first six weeks in office, often after requests by the regulated industries.
Trump vowed to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act.
Trump scaled back the Affordable Care Act's implementation through executive orders and expressed a desire to 'let Obamacare fail'.
Trump barred organizations that provide abortions or abortion referrals from receiving federal funds.
Trump's administration rolled back key components of the Obama administration's workplace protections against discrimination of LGBTQ people.
Trump's administration took an anti-marijuana position, revoking Obama-era policies that provided protections for states that legalized marijuana.

Trump is a long-time advocate of capital punishment, and his administration oversaw the federal government execute 13 prisoners, more than in the previous 56 years combined, ending a 17-year moratorium.

Trump granted 237 requests for clemency during his first term, with only 25 of them vetted by the Justice Department's Office of the Pardon Attorney, and the others granted to people with personal or political connections to him, his family, and his allies, or recommended by celebrities.
Trump described illegal immigration as an 'invasion' of the United States and drastically escalated immigration enforcement.
Trump implemented harsh policies against asylum seekers and deployed nearly 6,000 troops to the U.S.-Mexico border to stop illegal crossings.
Trump reduced the number of refugees admitted to record lows, from an annual limit of 110,000 before he took office to 15,000 in 2021.
Trump increased restrictions on granting permanent residency to immigrants needing public benefits.
Trump's refusal to sign any spending bill unless it allocated funding for the border wall resulted in the longest-ever federal government shutdown, for 35 days from December 2018 to January 2019.
Trump declared a national emergency on the southern border to divert $6.1 billion of funding to the border wall despite congressional disagreement.
Trump signed an executive order that denied entry to citizens from six Muslim-majority countries for four months and from Syria indefinitely.
The Trump administration had a policy of family separation that separated over 4,400 children from migrant parents at the U.S.-Mexico border.

Trump mandated that illegal immigrant families be detained together unless there is a concern of risk for the child, after initially blaming Democrats and insisting he could not stop the policy with an executive order.
Trump began a trade war with China, imposing tariffs and other trade barriers in an effort to force China to end unfair trade practices and intellectual property infringement.
Trump weakened the toughest U.S. sanctions imposed after the 2014 Russian annexation of Crimea.
Trump became the first sitting U.S. president to meet a North Korean leader, meeting Kim Jong Un in an effort to discuss denuclearization.
Talks on North Korean denuclearization broke down, and no agreement was reached.
Trump supported populist, neo-nationalist, and authoritarian governments, and his foreign policy was characterized by unpredictability, uncertainty, and inconsistency.
Trump criticized NATO allies and privately suggested that the U.S. should withdraw from NATO.
Trump signed legislation to increase funding for drug treatments in response to the opioid epidemic, but was widely criticized for failing to make a concrete strategy.
The Supreme Court allowed a revised travel ban to go into effect, which extended travel bans to North Koreans, Chadians, and some Venezuelan officials.
The Supreme Court upheld the travel ban, which had been revised to extend travel bans to North Koreans, Chadians, and some Venezuelan officials.
Trump's administration attempted to roll back anti-discrimination protections for transgender patients, but was halted by a federal judge.
The U.S. Supreme Court allowed Donald Trump's tax returns and other records to be released to the prosecutor for review by a grand jury after a lengthy court battle
The FBI launched Crossfire Hurricane, an investigation into possible links between Russia and Donald Trump's 2016 campaign.
Three U.S. intelligence agencies jointly stated with 'high confidence' that Russia interfered in the 2016 presidential election to favor Donald Trump.
Donald Trump dismissed Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) director James Comey, later stating he was concerned about Comey's role in the Trump-Russia investigations.
Donald Trump halted funding of the World Health Organization, after weeks of attacks to draw attention away from his slow response to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Mueller's final report found that Russia interfered in 2016 to favor Trump and that Trump and his campaign welcomed and encouraged the effort
Trump pressured Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy to investigate Joe and Hunter Biden in an attempt to gain an advantage in the 2020 presidential election
The House voted to impeach Trump for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress
Trump repeatedly refused to say whether he would accept the results of the 2020 presidential election if he lost and commit to a peaceful transition of power
The Senate acquitted Trump of abuse of power and obstruction of Congress

Donald Trump conducted a COVID-19 press briefing with members of the White House Coronavirus Task Force, despite initially ignoring public health warnings and calls for action from health officials within his administration.
Donald Trump signed into law the CARES Act, a $2.2 trillion bipartisan economic stimulus bill, the largest stimulus in U.S. history, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Trump declared victory in the presidential election before the results were known
Trump baselessly alleged election fraud after Joe Biden was projected the winner of the presidential election

Trump and his allies filed many lawsuits challenging the results of the presidential election, which were rejected by at least 86 judges in both state and federal courts for having no factual or legal basis
Donald Trump continued to make false claims about the 2020 election, which were commonly referred to as the 'big lie' by his critics.
Trump held a rally at which he called for the election to be overturned and urged his supporters to 'fight like hell' and 'take back our country' by marching to the Capitol

Trump's supporters formed a mob that broke into the Capitol building, disrupting certification and causing the evacuation of Congress, resulting in over 140 police officers injured and five people dead
The House charged Trump with incitement of insurrection for his role in the January 6 Capitol attack
The Senate acquitted Trump of incitement of insurrection
Trump and his supporters began using the term 'big lie' to refer to the 2020 election itself, despite his false claims about the election.

The Supreme Court, with Donald Trump's appointed justices, overturned Roe v. Wade, a decision that Trump had previously stated would happen 'automatically' if he were elected and given the opportunity to appoint two or three anti-abortion justices.
Trump's Trump Media & Technology Group (TMTG) launched a pro-Trump social media platform called Truth Social.

The U.S. House committee on the January 6 attack recommended criminal charges against Trump for obstructing an official proceeding, inciting or assisting an insurrection, and conspiracy to defraud the United States.
Trump's campaign began diverting 10% of donations to his leadership PAC
A grand jury in Fulton County, Georgia, indicted Trump on 13 charges, including racketeering, for his efforts to subvert the 2020 election in the state.
E. Jean Carroll's lawsuits against Trump for sexual abuse and defamation resulted in him being found liable and ordered to pay $5 million in one case and $83.3 million in the other.

Trump was convicted on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records, stemming from evidence that he booked Michael Cohen's hush-money payments to adult film actress Stormy Daniels as business expenses.
Trump's campaign had paid $100 million towards his legal bills by March 2024
Trump made increasingly violent and authoritarian statements during his campaign
Trump said he would weaponize the FBI and the Justice Department against his political opponents
Trump used harsher and more dehumanizing anti-immigrant rhetoric than during his presidency
Trump's rhetoric was described by some historians and scholars as authoritarian, fascist, and unlike anything a political candidate has ever said in American history
Trump mentioned 'rigged election' and 'election interference' earlier and more frequently than in the 2016 and 2020 campaigns and refused to commit to accepting the 2024 election results
Judge Aileen Cannon dismissed the case against Trump, ruling that Special Counsel Jack Smith's appointment as special prosecutor was unconstitutional
Donald Trump's administration asserted a constitutional right to ignore federal law in its justification of several actions, including refusing to enforce a federal ban on TikTok

Trump's second presidency has been described as having fewer guardrails against conflicts of interest than his first, breaking with decades of ethical norms and raising substantial corruption concerns
Trump maintained a publicly traded company, Trump Media & Technology Group, and diversified it into financial services while in office
Trump pursued new overseas real estate deals involving state-affiliated entities and had several branding and licensing deals selling Trump-branded merchandise while in office
Trump profited from events held at his hotels and golf courses and did not place his assets in a blind trust, as previous presidents had done
Trump launched, promoted, and personally benefited from two cryptocurrency tokens, $Trump and $Melania
Trump directly benefited from his family's cryptocurrency company, World Liberty Financial, which engaged in an unprecedented mixing of private enterprise and government policy
Trump implemented a hiring freeze across the federal government and ordered telework of federal employees to be discontinued within 30 days
Trump ordered a review of many career civil service positions with the intention of reclassifying them into at-will positions without job protections
Trump initiated mass job terminations of federal employees, which were described by legal experts as unprecedented or in violation of federal law

Trump ordered an end to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) projects in the federal government and rescinded Lyndon B. Johnson's 1965 Executive Order 11246, which had mandated that federal contractors take affirmative action to end racial discrimination

Trump's Department of Government Efficiency largely dismantled several federal agencies, including USAID and the Department of Education, unilaterally fired several thousand staff, and reduced administrative functions to statutory minimums
Trump threatened, signed executive actions, and ordered investigations into his political opponents, critics, and organizations aligned with the Democratic Party
Trump ended the post-Watergate norm of Justice Department independence, weaponizing it and agencies across the federal government to target his political enemies

Trump's actions against civil society were described by hundreds of legal experts and political scientists as authoritarian, contributing to democratic backsliding, and negatively impacting the rule of law
Trump's pardons and grants of clemency favored political allies and loyalists, and disproportionately pardoned 'the powerful, famous, well-connected and wealthy' accused of white-collar crime
Trump was given a no-penalty sentence, known as an unconditional discharge, for his conviction on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records
Trump became the oldest to assume the presidency and the first president with a felony conviction
Trump began his second term with attempts to expand presidential power, which is a defining characteristic of his second term
Trump issued more executive orders than any other U.S. president's first 100 days
By late February 2025, the Trump administration had fired more than 30,000 federal employees
Trump defied judges and the courts in roughly one third of all cases against him, actions which were described by legal experts as unprecedented for any presidential administration
As of mid-January 2026, Trump's orders and actions had been challenged in over 550 lawsuits nationwide

Trump ran full-page newspaper advertisements and insisted that a group of five black and Latino teenagers were guilty of raping a white woman in the Central Park jogger case, even after they were cleared of suspicion in 2002.

Trump granted clemency to all January 6 rioters convicted or charged on his first day in office, including those who violently attacked police, by pardoning more than 1,500 and commuting the sentences of 14.
Trump ordered agencies to stop enforcement of disfavored rules in an attempt at large-scale deregulation that legal experts described as illegal and contrary to decades of federal law.

Trump sought greater government control over private businesses and shifted away from traditional conservative free market orthodoxy, engaging in state capitalism by taking direct government equity stakes in multiple U.S. corporations.

Trump canceled and paused federal grants and made large cuts to scientific research, several of which were found by judges and the Government Accountability Office as being illegal and unconstitutional.
Trump and his administration's Make America Healthy Again agenda promoted anti-science and anti-vaccine activism, resulting in a resurgence of whooping cough and measles.
Trump relied on declaring national emergencies to justify hundreds of actions and bypass congressional approval or regulatory review.
Trump declared a national energy emergency, allowing the suspension of environmental regulations, loosening the rules for fossil fuel extraction and limiting renewable energy projects.
Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Paris Agreement on climate change during his second presidency.
Trump attributed societal problems to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives and wokeness, equating diversity with incompetence.
Trump reversed pro-diversity policies in the federal government.
Trump threatened cultural institutions and sixty universities on DEI and antisemitism grounds, and forced law firms to capitulate to his political agenda.
Trump expanded the domestic use of the military and ordered military deployments to several Democratic Party-led cities, and threatened to expand his deployments further.

Trump banned transgender people from the military, restricted or defunded gender-affirming healthcare, opposed inclusive language, censored research and education materials, targeted schools, universities, and cultural institutions accused of promoting what his government calls 'gender ideology', barred transgender athletes from sports, and required U.S. passports to state transgender people's sex assigned at birth.
Trump instructed Border Patrol agents to summarily deport illegal immigrants crossing the border and disabled the CBP One app that was being used to schedule border crossings.
Trump resumed the Remain in Mexico policy, designated drug cartels as terrorist groups, and ordered construction to be resumed on a border wall.
Trump revoked the parole status of immigrants who entered the U.S. under CBP One and ended humanitarian parole for Cubans, Haitians, Nicaraguans.
Trump targeted activists, legal immigrants, tourists, and students with visas who expressed criticism of his policies or pro-Palestinian advocacy.
Several American citizens were detained and deported under Trump's policies.
Trump used the Alien Enemies Act of 1798 to imprison illegal immigrants without trial at the Terrorism Confinement Center in El Salvador.

Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act into law, making the temporary tax cuts of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act permanent and adding additional tax deductions for a total of around $4.5 trillion, mostly benefiting the highest income brackets and costing people in the lowest income bracket $1,600 per year.

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act increased funding for national defense, deportations, the border wall, and Trump's proposed Golden Dome missile defense system, while removing tax credits for renewable energy sources and cutting funding for Medicaid and SNAP.
Trump imposed tariffs on most countries, including large tariffs on major trading partners China, Canada, and Mexico.
Trump's second term foreign policy was described as expansionist and imperialist.
Trump and Vice President Vance berated Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy in a highly contentious televised meeting.
Trump ordered the bombing of Iranian nuclear sites.
Trump's plan for a Gaza ceasefire deal between Israel and Hamas was signed, leading to the creation of the Board of Peace.
Trump's aggressive and hardline deportation campaign led to large nationwide protests and violent confrontations with migrants and protesters.
Trump authorized joint U.S./Israeli air strikes on Iran.
The Supreme Court ruled the tariffs Trump imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to be illegal.
Donald Trump posed as a Trump Organization official named 'John Barron' and called journalist Jonathan Greenberg, trying to get a higher ranking on the Forbes 400 list of wealthy Americans.

Donald Trump bragged about kissing and groping women without their consent in a 2005 'hot mic' recording, saying 'when you're a star, they let you do it. You can do anything. ... Grab 'em by the pussy'
Donald Trump advocated for birther conspiracy theories, helping to bring far-right ideas into the mainstream.
Donald Trump promoted the racist 'birther' conspiracy theory that Barack Obama, the first black U.S. president, was not born in the United States
Donald Trump's rhetoric has been associated with an increased incidence of hate crimes, and he has been accused of embracing extremism, conspiracy theories, and far-right militia movements
Trump's politics and rhetoric led to the formation of a political movement known as Trumpism, which has been compared to a cult of personality.
Trump frequently accused the press of bias, calling it the 'fake news media' and 'the enemy of the people'.
Donald Trump apologized for his 2005 comments about kissing and groping women without their consent, characterizing them as 'locker-room talk'
Trump condemned the Unite the Right rally, stating that there were 'very fine people on both sides', implying a moral equivalence between white supremacist demonstrators and counter-protesters.
Trump reportedly referred to El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, and African nations as 'shithole countries', remarks condemned as racist.
The first Trump presidency reduced formal press briefings from about one hundred in 2017 to two by 2019 and revoked the press passes of two White House reporters, which were restored by the courts.
The House of Representatives condemned Trump for racist remarks about four minority Democratic congresswomen.
26 women have publicly accused Donald Trump of sexual misconduct, including rape, kissing without consent, groping, looking under women's skirts, and walking in on naked teenage pageant contestants

Donald Trump refused to condemn the white supremacist Proud Boys during a 2020 presidential debate, and his comment 'Proud Boys, stand back and stand by' was said to have led to increased recruitment for the pro-Trump group
Donald Trump's disinformation about the COVID-19 pandemic delayed and weakened the national response to it
Donald Trump's attacks on mail-in ballots and other election practices weakened public faith in the integrity of the 2020 presidential election
Donald Trump promoted conspiracy theories about climate change denial, alleged Ukrainian interference in U.S. elections, and vaccines and autism.
After the 2020 presidential election, Trump promoted conspiracy theories for his defeat that were characterized as 'the big lie'.

A nationwide review identified at least 54 criminal cases, from August 2015 to April 2020, in which Donald Trump was invoked in direct connection with violence or threats of violence, mostly by white men and primarily against minorities
Twitter began to tag some of Trump's tweets with fact-checks in May 2020.

Federal law-enforcement officials used tear gas and other crowd control tactics to remove a largely peaceful crowd of lawful protesters from Lafayette Square, outside the White House, before Trump posed with a Bible for a photo op at the nearby St. John's Episcopal Church.
Twitter banned Trump after the January 6 attack.

Trump repeatedly voiced support for outlawing political dissent and criticism, and said that reporters should be prosecuted for not divulging confidential sources and media companies should possibly lose their broadcast licenses for unfavorable coverage of him.
Trump's 2024 campaign made extensive use of dehumanizing language and racial stereotypes.
Trump selected cabinet members with personal loyalty to him, with the focus on loyalty over subject-matter expertise.
Trump threatened the annexation of Greenland, including by military force or the imposition of tariffs on European allies.
Trump gave Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) access to many federal government agencies.
Trump pardoned former Honduran president Juan Orlando Hernández, who had been extradited to the U.S. in 2022 and sentenced to 45 years in prison for drug trafficking.
The U.S. captured Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro in a military raid in Venezuela and took him to New York, where he was charged with drug trafficking.
Donald Trump self-reported his net worth as minus $900 million.
Trump's Taj Mahal casino filed for bankruptcy
Trump's Castle and Plaza filed for bankruptcy
Trump revised the loan for his Plaza hotel
Trump's Plaza hotel bankruptcy plan was approved
Trump sold his Plaza hotel to Saudi and Asian investors
Trump Plaza casino stock began trading on the Big Board
Donald Trump self-reported his net worth as $10 billion.
Forbes estimated Donald Trump's net worth at $4.5 billion, based on interviews with more than 80 sources.
Donald Trump was named the 'King of Whoppers' by FactCheck.org due to his frequent misstatements during his campaign
PolitiFact named Donald Trump's campaign misstatements as the '2015 Lie of the Year'
It was reported that Donald Trump's companies had declared bankruptcy more than four times.
An investigation found that Trump had used his charity to spend other people's money
The Trump Foundation was ordered to stop fundraising by the New York attorney general's office

International approval ratings of U.S. leadership dropped from about 22 percent in a Gallup poll of 134 countries to just 16 percent lower than China's Xi Jinping and Russia's Vladimir Putin in a Pew Research poll of 13 countries during Trump's first term.
Donald Trump had a 15-year friendship with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
Persons who knew Donald Trump and Jeffrey Epstein at the time said they frequently 'hit on' and competed for women.
Donald Trump's longtime personal physician, Harold Bornstein, said Trump had dictated the contents of a letter stating he would 'be the healthiest individual ever elected to the presidency'.
Hundreds of people alleged that Donald Trump does not pay his bills.
It was reported that Donald Trump engaged in suspect tax schemes as he reaped riches from his father.
Trump was ranked as the fourth-worst president in C-SPAN's 2021 survey of presidential historians.
The Siena College Research Institute's 2022 survey ranked Trump as the third-worst president.
Trump launched social media platform Truth Social where he attracted a fraction of his Twitter following.
Twitter reinstated Trump's account in November 2022.
Donald Trump's administration did not release files relating to Jeffrey Epstein, despite Trump's promise to do so during the 2024 campaign.
Some of the files relating to Jeffrey Epstein were released, mostly heavily redacted.

Roughly three million additional pages of files relating to Jeffrey Epstein were released, including approximately 38,000 references to Donald Trump, his wife, Mar-a-Lago, and other Trump-related terms.
Donald Trump agreed to pay $25 million in the Trump University settlement
Trump issued an executive order scaling back parts of Obamacare
Donald Trump implemented a travel ban that caused chaos and protests at US airports, affecting refugees and immigrants
A federal judge temporarily halted Trump's order on immigration and refugees
Trump made it easier for the mentally ill to get guns when he rolled back an Obama regulation
Trump rolled back regulations on Wall Street, gun sellers, and polluters
Trump signed a new travel ban directive
A Hawaii judge halted Trump's new travel ban before it could go into effect
The Supreme Court partly reinstated Trump's travel ban, allowing it to go into effect for some individuals
Trump said he plans to 'let Obamacare fail'
Trump cut enrollment programs for Obamacare, ramping up sabotage of the healthcare law
Trump signed an executive order that would allow companies to bypass certain Obamacare requirements
Trump's administration announced it would stop making key payments to insurers under Obamacare
It was reported that Donald Trump had gained over $100 million from his Mar-a-Lago resort
The Trump Foundation agreed to dissolve due to allegations of illegal activity
Trump proposed cuts to climate and clean-energy programs
Donald Trump was ordered to pay $2 million to a collection of non-profits as part of a civil lawsuit
The Trump administration was accused of sidelining researchers and their work on science and climate change

Trump's fossil fuel agenda faced pushback from federal judges
Trump proclaimed 'the coal industry is back', despite evidence to the contrary
Trump misled the public about Russian meddling in the US election, claiming that 17 intelligence agencies did not need to agree on the matter
Trump extended his travel ban to include blocks on North Korea, Venezuela, and Chad
The Supreme Court allowed Trump's latest travel ban to go into full effect
The Supreme Court upheld Trump's travel ban
Trump and Democrats failed to reach an agreement to reopen the government, leading to a prolonged shutdown
Trump signed a bill to temporarily reopen the government after the longest shutdown in US history
Trump declared a national emergency to build a border wall, setting up a massive legal fight
The Mueller report laid out evidence of obstruction of justice by Trump, including attempts to influence the investigation and witness testimony
Trump used words like 'invasion' and 'killer' at rallies more than 500 times since 2017, according to a USA Today analysis
Trump virtually cut off refugees as he unleashed a tirade on immigrants
Trump ended the US role as a worldwide leader on refugees
Donald Trump cut refugee admissions to America to a record low
The Trump administration approved sanctions on Russia's Nord Stream 2 gas pipeline
A crackdown on immigrants who use public benefits took effect

Trump aides explored executive actions to curb voting by mail, which could potentially disenfranchise voters
